what: Step 1: Generating Messages for Each Document
title: Structured Question Answering from PDFs
text: First, prepare list messages PDFs folder applying llm_message() prompt first five pages document. Even though gpt-5.4 can process 128,000 tokens (roughly 80-90 pages English text), limit input five pages demonstration purposes; introduction usually enough get first overview paper. Attach PDFs pdf_file() .media argument. providers support native PDF rendering (Claude, Gemini), file sent binary, preserving images, tables, formatting. providers text extracted automatically wrapped <pdf> tags prompt. Pass .text_extract = TRUE always force text extraction regardless provider:
code: files <- list.files("aipapers", full.names = TRUE, pattern = "\\.pdf$")  document_tasks <- files |>   map(\(f) llm_message(     "Below are the first 5 pages of a document.      Summarise the document based on the provided schema.",     .media = pdf_file(f, pages = 1:5)   ))

what: Step 2: Defining the Schema for Structured Output
title: Structured Question Answering from PDFs
text: define schema outlines expected data types field model’s responses. enables model return answers consistent, structured format converts directly tibble. field_chr() returns text, field_dbl() returns numbers, field_fct() restricts allowed categories via .levels argument. Setting .vector = TRUE field returns list values; keep flat structure document maps single-row tibble. name special field providing identifier schema.
code: document_schema <- tidyllm_schema(   name             = "DocumentAnalysisSchema",   Title            = field_chr("The full title of the provided document"),   Authors          = field_chr("A semicolon-separated list of authors"),   SuggestedFilename = field_chr("Suggest a filename in the format \"ReleaseYear_Author_etal_ShortTitle.pdf\". Use the publication year if available; otherwise, use XXXX."),   Type             = field_fct("Is the document a Policy Report or a Research Paper based on its style, structure, and purpose?",                                .levels = c("Policy", "Research")),   Empirics         = field_chr("A 100 word description of empirical methods used, including data collection, statistical techniques, or analysis methods mentioned."),   Theory           = field_chr("A 100 word outline of the primary theoretical framework or models discussed, if any."),   MainPoint        = field_chr("A one-sentence summary of the main point raised"),   Contribution     = field_chr("A short explanation of the main contributions claimed in the document"),   KeyCitations     = field_chr("The four most frequently cited or critical references in the first 5 pages") )

what: Step 3: Running the Analysis on a Sample Document
title: Structured Question Answering from PDFs
text: test setup, run analysis single document chat() schema:
code: example_task <- document_tasks[[1]] |>   chat(openai(.json_schema = document_schema,               .model       = "gpt-5.4"))

what: Step 4: Extracting and Formatting the Results
title: Structured Question Answering from PDFs
text: get_reply_data() extracts model’s structured response: model returns named list matching schema, as_tibble() converts single row. can also inspect token usage: current gpt-5.4 batch pricing per-document cost cents; processing folder 50 papers costs well dollar.
code: get_reply_data(example_task) ## $Title ## [1] "A Method to Link Advances in Artificial Intelligence to Occupational Abilities" ##  ## $Authors ## [1] "Edward W. Felten; Manav Raj; Robert Seamans" ##  ## $SuggestedFilename ## [1] "2018_Felten_etal_AILinkOccupations.pdf" ##  ## $Type ## [1] "Research" ##  ## $Empirics ## [1] "The study employs two main databases: the Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF) AI Progress Measurement dataset and the Occupational Information Network (O*NET). The EFF dataset tracks task-specific AI performance metrics across various categories from 2010 to 2015, while O*NET provides contemporary occupational definitions. The authors calculate the correlation between AI advancements and occupational changes using an analysis of variance methodology to derive impact scores for occupations." ##  ## $Theory ## [1] "The theoretical framework relies on linking advancements in various AI fields (e.g., image recognition) to a set of 52 abilities outlined by O*NET. The authors construct a matrix correlating EFF AI categories to O*NET abilities, assessing how AI impacts specific skills important for different occupations, theoretically grounded in human capital and job task frameworks." ##  ## $MainPoint ## [1] "The paper introduces a novel methodology to quantitatively assess the impact of AI advancements on occupational abilities, facilitating better understanding for researchers and policymakers." ##  ## $Contribution ## [1] "This research contributes a systematic approach for linking AI advancements to occupational changes, enhancing the understanding of AI's role in labor markets and the skills needed across different jobs. The methodology allows for further analysis of the varying impacts of AI on occupations, potentially aiding policy development." ##  ## $KeyCitations ## [1] "Autor and Handel (2013); Brynjolfsson et al. (2018); Acemoglu and Restrepo (2017); Frey and Osborne (2017)" get_metadata(example_task) ## # A tibble: 1 × 7 ##   model  timestamp           prompt_tokens completion_tokens total_tokens stream ##   <chr>  <dttm>                      <int>             <int>        <int> <lgl>  ## 1 gpt-5… 2026-03-16 10:00:00          4265               337         4602 FALSE  ## # ℹ 1 more variable: api_specific <list>

what: Step 5: Scaling Up to a Whole Batch of Papers
title: Structured Question Answering from PDFs
text: confirming single-document analysis works, extend workflow process full batch. Batch APIs Anthropic OpenAI offer 50% savings compared single requests, batch rate limits separate standard per-model limits don’t affect regular quota. output send_batch() contains input message list plus batch metadata (batch_id attribute unique per-message identifiers). Batches processed within 24 hours; example completed 10 minutes. ⚠️ Note: Save RDS file disk. acts checkpoint, letting reload check batch status retrieve results across R sessions without resubmitting batch. Check whether batch completed: can also list OpenAI batches list_batches(openai()) view OpenAI batches dashboard.
code: document_tasks |>   send_batch(openai(.json_schema = document_schema,                     .model       = "gpt-5.4")) |>   write_rds("document_batch.rds") read_rds("document_batch.rds") |>   check_job(openai())

title: Structured Question Answering from PDFs

what: Gemini and Claude for Image-Heavy PDFs
title: Structured Question Answering from PDFs
text: use pdf_file() gemini() claude(), PDF sent binary rather extracted text. means model sees actual layout, tables, charts, scanned pages, just raw characters. text-workflows provider, pass .text_extract = TRUE pdf_file() force text extraction skip binary encoding entirely. large frequently reused documents can also upload provider’s servers upload_file() reference returned handle messages via .files. avoids re-sending binary every request. Provider file APIs often accept broader range formats beyond PDFs: Gemini supports video, audio, various document types; OpenAI accepts Office files (DOCX, PPTX, XLSX), source code, ZIP archives, . makes upload_file() useful workflows go beyond plain PDFs. See Working Files Media article full walkthrough file upload workflow across providers.
code: report <- upload_file(claude(), .path = "annual_report.pdf")  llm_message("Summarise the key risks.", .files = report) |>   chat(claude())

text: Local models via ollama() llamacpp() strong choice privacy-sensitive documents, data leaves machine. Modern models like qwen3.5 support context window 262,144 tokens principle; practical limit typical laptop RAM. 16 GB machine 9B model Q4 quantisation already uses roughly 6 GB weights, leaving limited headroom long contexts inference slows crawl system starts swapping. academic papers first five pages fit comfortably within laptop hardware can handle. longer documents, limit page range reduce context window explicitly via .num_ctx parameter ollama(). ⚠️ Note: using paid remote APIs, consider data privacy, especially sensitive documents. Local models via ollama() llamacpp() keep processing -device.

title: Get Started
text: tidyllm R package providing unified interface interacting various large language model APIs. vignette guides basic setup usage tidyllm.

title: Get Started
text: install tidyllm CRAN: install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("tidyllm") devtools::install_github("edubruell/tidyllm")

title: Get Started
text: Set API keys environment variables. easiest way add .Renviron file (run usethis::edit_r_environ()) restart R: set temporarily session:
code: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-key-here" OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key-here" Sys.setenv(OPENAI_API_KEY = "your-key-here")

title: Get Started
text: tidyllm supports local inference via Ollama llama.cpp. run entirely machine; API key required. Ollama, install ollama.com pull model: llama.cpp, see Local Models article tidyllm website step--step setup guide.
code: ollama_download_model("qwen3.5:4b")

title: Get Started
text: tidyllm built around message-centric interface. Interactions work message history created llm_message() passed verbs like chat(): API interactions follow verb + provider pattern: Verbs define action: chat(), embed(), send_batch(), check_batch(), fetch_batch(), list_batches(), list_models(), deep_research(), check_job(), fetch_job(), upload_file(), list_files(), file_info(), delete_file() Providers specify API: openai(), claude(), gemini(), ollama(), mistral(), groq(), perplexity(), deepseek(), voyage(), openrouter(), llamacpp(), azure_openai() Provider-specific functions like openai_chat() claude_chat() also work directly expose full range parameters API.
code: library(tidyllm)  conversation <- llm_message("What is the capital of France?") |>   chat(claude())  conversation ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## What is the capital of France? ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## The capital of France is Paris. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- # Continue the conversation with a different provider conversation <- conversation |>   llm_message("What's a famous landmark in this city?") |>   chat(openai())  get_reply(conversation) ## [1] "A famous landmark in Paris is the Eiffel Tower."

title: Get Started
text: Attach images, PDFs, audio, video message using .media argument llm_message(). Pass single media object list :  Send multiple images one message passing list. list can also mix different media types; example, image alongside PDF audio file alongside chart. Gemini handles mixed-type lists flexibly: audio video, use audio_file() video_file(). Gemini supports natively; llama.cpp supports audio compatible models (Ultravox, Qwen2.5-Omni, Qwen3-Omni); OpenRouter routes audio video underlying models accept :
code: # Single image image_description <- llm_message("Describe this picture. Can you guess where it was taken?",                                   .media = img("picture.jpeg")) |>   chat(openai(.model = "gpt-5.4"))  get_reply(image_description) ## [1] "The picture shows a beautiful landscape with a lake, mountains, and a town nestled below. The area appears lush and green, with agricultural fields visible. This scenery is reminiscent of northern Italy, particularly around Lake Garda." llm_message("Compare these two charts.",             .media = list(img("chart_a.png"), img("chart_b.png"))) |>   chat(claude())  # Mixed types in one message llm_message("Does this figure match the results described in the paper?",             .media = list(img("figure.png"), pdf_file("paper.pdf", pages = 1:5))) |>   chat(gemini()) # Audio transcription or analysis llm_message("Transcribe and summarise this recording.",             .media = audio_file("interview.mp3")) |>   chat(gemini())  # Video understanding llm_message("Describe what happens in this clip.",             .media = video_file("demo.mp4")) |>   chat(gemini())

title: Get Started
text: Pass PDF pdf_file() attach .media. default file sent binary providers support native PDF rendering (Claude, Gemini), text automatically extracted fallback others: Restrict specific pages pages argument: large frequently reused documents, upload provider’s Files API reference handle message: File management verbs currently supported Claude, Gemini, OpenAI. Files uploaded stored specific provider’s servers; file uploaded Claude used Gemini OpenAI. Supported file types vary provider: Gemini accepts wide range including video, audio, images, PDFs, various document formats. OpenAI accepts 80+ formats including Office documents (PPTX, DOCX, XLSX), source code, ZIP archives, . Claude focuses PDFs images. Always check provider’s current documentation full list.
code: llm_message("Summarize the key points from this document.",             .media = pdf_file("die_verwandlung.pdf")) |>   chat(claude()) ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## Summarize the key points from this document. ##  -> [pdf: die_verwandlung.pdf]  ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## The story centres on Gregor Samsa, who wakes up transformed ## into a giant insect. Unable to work, he becomes isolated ## while his family struggles. Eventually Gregor dies, and his ## relieved family looks ahead to a better future. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- pdf_file("report.pdf", pages = 1:5) report <- upload_file(claude(), .path = "annual_report.pdf")  llm_message("What are the key risks mentioned?", .files = report) |>   chat(claude())  # Manage uploaded files list_files(claude()) file_info(claude(), report) delete_file(claude(), report)

title: Get Started
text: Use .f capture console output function .capture_plot include last plot:
code: library(tidyverse)  ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +   geom_point() +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = "y ~ x") +   labs(x = "Weight", y = "Miles per gallon") llm_message("Analyze this plot and data summary:",             .capture_plot = TRUE,             .f = ~{summary(mtcars)}) |>   chat(claude()) ## Warning: The `.imagefile` argument of `llm_message()` is deprecated as of tidyllm 0.5.0. ## ℹ Please use the `.media` argument instead. ## ℹ Replace with .media = img(path). ## This warning is displayed once per session. ## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was ## generated. ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## Analyze this plot and data summary: ##  -> [image: file1568f6c1b4565.png] [rconsole: RConsole.txt]  ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## The scatter plot shows a clear negative correlation between ## weight and fuel efficiency. Heavier cars consistently ## achieve lower mpg, with the linear trend confirming this ## relationship. Variability around the line suggests other ## factors, engine size and transmission, also play a role. ## --------------------------------------------------------------

title: Get Started
text: get_reply() retrieves assistant text message history. Use index access specific reply, omit get last: Convert message history (without attachments) tibble as_tibble(): get_metadata() returns token counts, model names, API-specific metadata assistant replies: api_specific list column holds provider-metadata citations (Perplexity), thinking traces (Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek), grounding information (Gemini).

conversation <- llm_message("Imagine a German address.") |>   chat(groq()) |>   llm_message("Imagine another address.") |>   chat(claude())  conversation ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## Imagine a German address. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## Herr Müller ## Musterstraße 12 ## 53111 Bonn ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## Imagine another address. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## Frau Schmidt ## Fichtenweg 78 ## 42103 Wuppertal ## -------------------------------------------------------------- conversation |> get_reply(1)   # first reply ## [1] "Herr Müller
Musterstraße 12
53111 Bonn" conversation |> get_reply()    # last reply (default) ## [1] "Frau Schmidt
Fichtenweg 78
42103 Wuppertal" conversation |> as_tibble() ## # A tibble: 5 × 2 ##   role      content                                        ##   <chr>     <chr>                                          ## 1 system    "You are a helpful assistant"                  ## 2 user      "Imagine a German address."                    ## 3 assistant "Herr Müller
Musterstraße 12
53111 Bonn"     ## 4 user      "Imagine another address."                     ## 5 assistant "Frau Schmidt
Fichtenweg 78
42103 Wuppertal" conversation |> get_metadata() ## # A tibble: 2 × 6 ##   model  timestamp           prompt_tokens completion_tokens ##   <chr>  <dttm>                      <int>             <int> ## 1 groq-… 2025-11-08 14:25:43            20                45 ## 2 claud… 2025-11-08 14:26:02            80                40 ## # ℹ 2 more variables: total_tokens <int>, ## #   api_specific <list>

title: Get Started
text: Use list_models() query models provider offers:
code: list_models(openai()) list_models(openrouter())   # 300+ models across providers list_models(ollama())       # models installed locally

title: Get Started
text: tidyllm supports JSON schema enforcement models return structured, machine-readable data. Use tidyllm_schema() field helpers define schema: field_chr(): text field_dbl(): numeric field_lgl(): boolean field_fct(.levels = ...): enumeration field_object(...): nested object (supports .vector = TRUE arrays) Extract parsed result R list get_reply_data(): Set .vector = TRUE field_object() get outputs unpack directly data frame: ellmer package installed, can use ellmer type objects (ellmer::type_string(), ellmer::type_object(), etc.) directly field definitions tidyllm_schema() .json_schema argument.
code: person_schema <- tidyllm_schema(   first_name = field_chr("A male first name"),   last_name  = field_chr("A common last name"),   occupation = field_chr("A quirky occupation"),   address    = field_object(     "The person's home address",     street  = field_chr("Street name"),     number  = field_dbl("House number"),     city    = field_chr("A large city"),     zip     = field_dbl("Postal code"),     country = field_fct("Country", .levels = c("Germany", "France"))   ) )  profile <- llm_message("Imagine a person profile matching the schema.") |>   chat(openai(), .json_schema = person_schema)  profile ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## Imagine a person profile matching the schema. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## {"first_name":"Julien","last_name":"Martin","occupation":"Gondola ## Repair Specialist","address":{"street":"Rue de ## Rivoli","number":112,"city":"Paris","zip":75001,"country":"France"}} ## -------------------------------------------------------------- profile |> get_reply_data() |> str() ## List of 4 ##  $ first_name: chr "Julien" ##  $ last_name : chr "Martin" ##  $ occupation: chr "Gondola Repair Specialist" ##  $ address   :List of 5 ##   ..$ street : chr "Rue de Rivoli" ##   ..$ number : int 112 ##   ..$ city   : chr "Paris" ##   ..$ zip    : int 75001 ##   ..$ country: chr "France" llm_message("Imagine five people.") |>   chat(openai(),        .json_schema = tidyllm_schema(          person = field_object(            first_name = field_chr(),            last_name  = field_chr(),            occupation = field_chr(),            .vector    = TRUE          )        )) |>   get_reply_data() |>   bind_rows() ##   first_name last_name          occupation ## 1      Alice   Johnson  Software Developer ## 2     Robert  Anderson    Graphic Designer ## 3      Maria  Gonzalez      Data Scientist ## 4       Liam  O'Connor Mechanical Engineer ## 5     Sophia       Lee      Content Writer

title: Get Started
text: Common arguments like .model, .temperature, .json_schema can set directly verbs like chat(). Provider-specific arguments go provider function: argument appears chat() provider function, chat() takes precedence. common argument supported chosen provider, chat() raises error rather silently ignoring .
code: # Common args in the verb llm_message("Write a haiku about tidyllm.") |>   chat(ollama(), .temperature = 0)  # Provider-specific args in the provider llm_message("Hello") |>   chat(ollama(.ollama_server = "http://my-server:11434"), .temperature = 0)

title: Get Started
text: Define R functions model can call conversation. Wrap tidyllm_tool(): tool provided, model can request execution, tidyllm runs R session, result fed back automatically. Multi-turn tool loops (model makes several tool calls) handled transparently. use ellmer, convert existing ellmer tool definitions ellmer_tool(): detailed guide, see Tool Use article tidyllm website.
code: get_current_time <- function(tz, format = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") {   format(Sys.time(), tz = tz, format = format, usetz = TRUE) }  time_tool <- tidyllm_tool(   .f          = get_current_time,   .description = "Returns the current time in a specified timezone.",   tz     = field_chr("The timezone identifier, e.g. 'Europe/Berlin'."),   format = field_chr("strftime format string. Default: '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'.") )  llm_message("What time is it in Stuttgart right now?") |>   chat(openai(), .tools = time_tool) ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## What time is it in Stuttgart right now? ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## The current time in Stuttgart (Europe/Berlin) is 2025-03-03 ## 09:51:22 CET. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- library(ellmer) btw_tool <- ellmer_tool(btw::btw_tool_files_list_files) llm_message("List files in the R/ folder.") |>   chat(claude(), .tools = btw_tool)

title: Get Started
text: Generate vector representations text embed(). output tibble one row per input: Voyage AI supports multimodal embeddings, meaning text images share vector space: voyage_rerank() re-orders documents relevance query:
code: c("What is the meaning of life?",   "How much wood would a woodchuck chuck?",   "How does the brain work?") |>   embed(ollama()) ## # A tibble: 3 × 2 ##   input                                  embeddings  ##   <chr>                                  <list>      ## 1 What is the meaning of life?           <dbl [384]> ## 2 How much wood would a woodchuck chuck? <dbl [384]> ## 3 How does the brain work?               <dbl [384]> list("tidyllm logo", img("docs/logo.png")) |>   embed(voyage()) voyage_rerank(   "best R package for LLMs",   c("tidyllm", "ellmer", "httr2", "rvest") )

title: Get Started
text: Batch APIs (Claude, OpenAI, Mistral, Groq, Gemini) process large numbers requests overnight roughly half cost standard calls. Use send_batch() submit, check_batch() poll status, fetch_batch() retrieve results: check_job() fetch_job() type-dispatching aliases; work batch objects Perplexity research jobs (see deep_research() ).
code: # Submit a batch and save the job handle to disk glue::glue("Write a poem about {x}", x = c("cats", "dogs", "hamsters")) |>   purrr::map(llm_message) |>   send_batch(claude()) |>   saveRDS("claude_batch.rds") # Check status readRDS("claude_batch.rds") |>   check_batch(claude()) ## # A tibble: 1 × 8 ##   batch_id          status created_at          expires_at          req_succeeded ##   <chr>             <chr>  <dttm>              <dttm>                      <int> ## 1 msgbatch_02A1B2C… ended  2025-11-01 10:30:00 2025-11-02 10:30:00             3 ## # ℹ 3 more variables: req_errored <int>, req_expired <int>, req_canceled <int> # Fetch completed results conversations <- readRDS("claude_batch.rds") |>   fetch_batch(claude())  poems <- purrr::map_chr(conversations, get_reply)

title: Get Started
text: long-horizon research tasks, deep_research() runs extended web search synthesis. Perplexity’s sonar-deep-research model currently supported:
code: # Blocking: waits for completion, returns an LLMMessage result <- llm_message("Compare Rust and Go for systems programming in 2025.") |>   deep_research(perplexity())  get_reply(result) get_metadata(result)$api_specific[[1]]$citations # Background mode: returns a job handle immediately job <- llm_message("Summarise recent EU AI Act developments.") |>   deep_research(perplexity(), .background = TRUE)  check_job(job)           # poll status result <- fetch_job(job) # retrieve when complete

title: Get Started
text: Stream reply tokens console generated .stream = TRUE: Streaming useful monitoring long responses interactively. production data-analysis workflows non-streaming mode preferred provides complete metadata reliable.
code: llm_message("Write a short poem about R.") |>   chat(claude(), .stream = TRUE)

title: Get Started
text: getting started local models (Ollama llama.cpp), see Local Models article tidyllm website.

title: Get Started
text: Avoid specifying provider every call setting options:

title: Classifying Texts with tidyllm
text: compare prompts models systematically, write classifier takes occupation description, ground-truth code, prompt text, prompt ID, provider, model name arguments:
code: classify_occupation_grid <- function(occupation, occ2, prompt, prompt_id, provider, model) {   glue("Classifying: {model} - {prompt_id} - {occupation}") |> cat("\n")    valid_codes <- as.character(occ_codes$occ2)    classification <- tryCatch({     reply <- llm_message(prompt) |>       chat(provider(.model = model, .temperature = 0)) |>       get_reply() |>       str_squish()      if (reply %in% valid_codes) as.integer(reply) else 98L   }, error = function(e) 97L)    tibble(     occupation_open = occupation,     occ2_predict    = classification,     occ2_truth      = occ2,     model           = model,     prompt_id       = prompt_id   ) }

what: Defining the Prompt and Model Grid
title: Classifying Texts with tidyllm
text: test three prompt variants two local models run via ollama(). llama3.2 fast 3B model; gemma2 stronger 9B model slower. expand_grid() creates every combination prompts, models, training rows: Running full grid: Note: Runs like can take laptop hardware. Saving intermediate results write_rds() via pwalk() protects losing progress something goes wrong.
code: prompts <- tibble(   prompt = c(     'Classify this occupation response from a survey: {occupation}      Pick one of the following numerical codes from this list.      Respond only with the code!      {numerical_code_list}',      'Classify this occupation response from a survey: {occupation}      Pick one of the following numerical codes from this list.      Respond only with the code!      {numerical_code_list}      If this does not look like a valid occupation response reply with just 99',      'Classify this occupation: {occupation}.      Respond only with one of the following codes:      {numerical_code_list}'   ),   prompt_id = 1:3 )  grid <- expand_grid(train_data, prompts, model = c("llama3.2", "gemma2")) |>   arrange(model) |>   rename(occupation = occupation_open) |>   rowwise() |>   mutate(prompt = glue(prompt)) |>   ungroup() |>   select(model, occupation, occ2, prompt_id, prompt)  nrow(grid) ## [1] 1854 grid_results <- grid |>   pmap_dfr(classify_occupation_grid, provider = ollama) ## Classifying: gemma2 - 1 - Computer network technician ## Classifying: gemma2 - 2 - Educational support ## Classifying: gemma2 - 3 - Fine Carpentry ## Classifying: ...

title: Classifying Texts with tidyllm
text: use yardstick evaluate grid results. Factor-encoding codes lets accuracy() work directly predictions:  main findings: Gemma2 consistently outperforms llama3.2, even gemma2’s best result (79.1% Prompt 1) falls well short Claude’s 98% accuracy manual validation. simplified Prompt 3 hurts smaller model disproportionately; llama3.2 drops low 20%, gemma2 degrades gracefully. Let us look confusion matrix best gemma2 configuration see errors concentrate:  Errors cluster around management occupations missing-occupation cases. single easy fix, makes good stopping point: already know Claude’s 98% performance holds unseen data, practical choice run full dataset send_batch(claude()).
code: library(yardstick) ##  ## Attaching package: 'yardstick' ## The following object is masked from 'package:readr': ##  ##     spec  gr_factors <- grid_results |>   mutate(across(starts_with("occ2_"),     ~factor(.x,       levels = c(occ_codes$occ2, 97, 98, 99),       labels = c(occ_codes$occ_title, "APIFAIL", "INVALID", "MISSING")     )   )) accuracy <- gr_factors |>   group_by(prompt_id, model) |>   accuracy(truth = occ2_truth, estimate = occ2_predict)  accuracy |>   ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(prompt_id), y = .estimate, fill = model)) +   geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +   labs(title = "Accuracy by Prompt and Model", x = "Prompt ID", y = "Accuracy", fill = "") +   theme_bw(22) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(), limits = c(0, 1)) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") shorten_label <- function(x) {   x |>     str_remove(" Occupations$") |>     str_wrap(width = 20) }  conf_mat_data <- gr_factors |>   filter(prompt_id == 1, model == "gemma2") |>   mutate(across(starts_with("occ2_"), ~fct_relabel(.x, shorten_label)))  conf_mat <- conf_mat_data |>   conf_mat(truth = occ2_truth, estimate = occ2_predict)  autoplot(conf_mat, type = "heatmap") +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#E41A1C") +   ggtitle("Confusion Matrix: gemma2, Prompt 1") +   theme_bw(16) +   theme(     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 12),     axis.text.y = element_text(size = 12)   ) ## Scale for fill is already present. ## Adding another scale for fill, which will replace the existing scale.

Respond only with the code!      {numerical_code_list}'   )))  batch_job <- all_tasks |>   send_batch(claude(.temperature = 0))  write_rds(batch_job, "full_batch_job.rds") final_results <- read_rds("full_batch_job.rds") |>   fetch_job(claude()) |>   map_chr(get_reply) |>   parse_number()  tibble(occupation = distinct_occupations$occupation, occ2 = final_results) |>   left_join(occ_data, by = c("occupation" = "occupation_open")) |>   left_join(occ_codes, by = "occ2")

title: Classifying Texts with tidyllm
text: article demonstrated practical workflow LLM-based text classification tidyllm: One message per item parse_number() strong, simple baseline; assume adding structure automatically improves accuracy. Batch processing via send_batch() / fetch_job() cuts costs half change prompt logic. train/test split yardstick metrics make model prompt comparisons reproducible honest. Accuracy evaluation manually reviewed ground truth essential; many apparent errors genuinely ambiguous cases ontology uncertain. Local models via ollama() viable privacy-sensitive work; 79% accuracy gemma2 Prompt 1 may acceptable depending use-case requirements.

what: Semantic Search in Economics Paper Abstracts
title: Embedding Models in tidyllm
text: demonstrate embeddings action, implement semantic search dataset 22,960 economics paper abstracts published 2010 2024. Instead relying keyword matching, use embeddings find papers similar topics based underlying meaning. dataset ready, use tidyllm generate embeddings perform semantic search find papers similar target_abstract paper commuting high-speed rail Heuermann Schmieder (2018). task use mxbai-embed-large model, available via Ollama ollama pull mxbai-embed-large. important choose embedding model carefully upfront, model produces unique numerical representations interchangeable models. Embedding APIs also available voyage(), mistral(), gemini(), openai() (well azure_openai()).
code: library(tidyverse) library(tidyllm) library(here)  abstracts <- read_rds("abstracts_data.rds") print(abstracts, width = 60) ## # A tibble: 22,960 × 8 ##    year  journal  authors volume firstpage lastpage abstract ##    <chr> <chr>    <chr>   <chr>  <chr>     <chr>    <chr>    ##  1 2024  Journal… Bauer,… 22     2075      2107     This pa… ##  2 2019  The Rev… Karlan… 86     1704      1746     We use … ##  3 2022  Journal… Corset… 20     513       548      We stud… ##  4 2018  The Rev… Anagol… 85     1971      2004     We stud… ##  5 2024  America… Thores… 16     447       79       This pa… ##  6 2024  Journal… Ren, Y… 238    NA        NA       The rap… ##  7 2013  The Rev… Adhvar… 95     725       740      A key p… ##  8 2022  Econome… Brooks… 90     2187      2214     If expe… ##  9 2011  Health … Fletch… 20     553       570      We exam… ## 10 2010  Journal… Rohwed… 24     119       38       Early r… ## # ℹ 22,950 more rows ## # ℹ 1 more variable: pdf_link <chr>  target_abstract <- "We use the expansion of the high-speed rail network in Germany as a natural experiment to examine the causal effect of reductions in commuting time between regions on the commuting decisions of workers and their choices regarding where to live and where to work. We exploit three key features in this setting: i) investment in high-speed rail has, in some cases dramatically, reduced travel times between regions, ii) several small towns were connected to the high-speed rail network only for political reasons, and iii) high-speed trains have left the transportation of goods unaffected. Combining novel information on train schedules and the opening of high-speed rail stations with panel data on all workers in Germany, we show that a reduction in travel time by one percent raises the number of commuters between regions by 0.25 percent. This effect is mainly driven by workers changing jobs to smaller cities while keeping their place of residence in larger ones. Our findings support the notion that benefits from infrastructure investments accrue in particular to peripheral regions, which gain access to a large pool of qualified workers with a preference for urban life. We find that the introduction of high-speed trains led to a modal shift towards rail transportation in particular on medium distances between 150 and 400 kilometers."

what: Step 2: Computing Embeddings for the Entire Abstract Corpus
title: Embedding Models in tidyllm
text: working large corpus like 22,960 abstracts, embedding entries single pass impractical. Commercial APIs typically cap inputs per request 50 100; local APIs face memory compute constraints. batch data groups 200 write results disk go. MacBook Pro M1 Pro completes approximately 25 minutes writes 207 MB data. compare multiple target abstracts collection, need embed corpus . function finishes, load precomputed embeddings:
code: generate_abstract_embeddings <- function(abstracts) {   embedding_batches <- tibble(abstract = abstracts) |>     group_by(batch = floor(1:n() / 200) + 1) |>     group_split()    n_batches <- length(embedding_batches)   glue("Processing {n_batches} batches of 200 abstracts") |> cat("\n")    embedding_batches |>     walk(\(batch) {       batch_number <- pull(batch, batch) |> unique()       glue("Generate Text Embeddings for Abstract Batch: {batch_number}/{n_batches}") |> cat("\n")        batch$abstract |>         embed(ollama(.model = "mxbai-embed-large:latest")) |>         write_rds(here("embedded_abstracts", paste0(batch_number, ".rds")))     }) }  abstracts |>   pull(abstract) |>   generate_abstract_embeddings() ## Processing 115 batches of 200 abstracts ## Generate Text Embeddings for Abstract Batch: 1/115 ## Generate Text Embeddings for Abstract Batch: 2/115 ## Generate Text Embeddings for Abstract Batch: 3/115 ## ... embedded_abstracts <- here("embedded_abstracts") |>   dir() |>   map_dfr(\(f) read_rds(here("embedded_abstracts", f)))

title: Embedding Models in tidyllm
text: embeddings precomputed, perform semantic search using cosine similarity. Cosine similarity measures similarity two vectors computing cosine angle ; ranges -1 (opposite directions) 1 (identical directions): cosine_similarity(𝐚,𝐛)=∑=1naibi∑=1nai2⋅∑=1nbi2 \text{cosine\_similarity}(\mathbf{}, \mathbf{b}) = \frac{\sum_{=1}^n a_i b_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{=1}^n a_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{=1}^n b_i^2}} Vectors 90 degrees (orthogonal, semantic overlap) cosine similarity 0; vectors pointing direction similarity close 1. express simple function: apply find 10 similar abstracts corpus: ten similar articles corpus: Top 10 Similar Abstracts Unsurprisingly, top result target abstract . remaining results strongly align target’s focus transportation infrastructure economic effects; second result discusses German high-speed rail, third fourth cover Japan’s Shinkansen China’s HSR network. illustrates model’s ability capture semantic connections across diverse geographic policy contexts.
code: cosine_similarity <- function(a, b) {   sum(a * b) / (sqrt(sum(a^2)) * sqrt(sum(b^2))) } top10_similar <- embedded_abstracts |>   mutate(cosine_sim = map_dbl(embeddings,                               \(e) cosine_similarity(e, target_tbl$embeddings[[1]]))) |>   arrange(desc(cosine_sim)) |>   slice(1:10) |>   rename(abstract = input) |>   left_join(abstracts |> select(year, authors, journal, abstract), by = "abstract") |>   select(-embeddings)

title: Embedding Models in tidyllm
text: Embedding-based search fast large corpora always precise; cosine similarity scores can noisy query documents differ style length. Reranking complements retrieval step: given short candidate list, reranker scores document query powerful cross-attention model. voyage_rerank() wraps Voyage’s /v1/rerank endpoint returns tibble sorted relevance score: typical workflow combines : embed full corpus , retrieve top 50 candidates cosine similarity, rerank surface best 5 10. keeps compute costs low improving final result quality.
code: candidates <- top10_similar$abstract  voyage_rerank(   .query     = target_abstract,   .documents = candidates,   .model     = "rerank-2",   .top_k     = 5 ) ## # A tibble: 5 × 3 ##   index relevance_score document                                                 ##   <int>           <dbl> <chr>                                                    ## 1     0           0.921 We study the economic effects of German High-Speed Rail… ## 2     4           0.887 This paper examines how Shinkansen expansion affected... ## 3     1           0.854 We estimate the effect of China's high-speed rail netwo… ## 4     7           0.731 Infrastructure investment and regional labor markets...  ## 5     2           0.698 Commuting zones and the spatial structure of local labo…

title: Embedding Models in tidyllm
text: Embeddings open door wide range analytical techniques beyond semantic search: Clustering topic discovery: Methods like K-means hierarchical clustering can automatically group texts themes, useful exploratory research large corpora without predefined labels. Dimensionality reduction visualisation: PCA, t-SNE, UMAP project embeddings 2D 3D, revealing clusters, trends, outliers glance. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG): embedding search retrieves semantically similar documents, appended model’s prompt ground responses relevant evidence. Supporting qualitative research workflows: discussed Kugler et al. (2023), embeddings can automate coding steps qualitative research, though models can still struggle implicit references closely related themes human researchers handle naturally.

text: Chat default model (qwen3.5:4b) using standard tidyllm verb pattern: .num_ctx parameter controls much text model can read , known context window. Text measured tokens, roughly three-quarters word . function default 2048 tokens, quite short much smaller modern models like Qwen3.5 actually support. long documents, increase fit input: embeddings, use embed() verb. Embeddings convert text numeric vectors capture meaning, making useful similarity search clustering. default embedding model qwen3-embedding:0.6b, purpose-built model task:
code: reply <- llm_message("What is the difference between a tibble and a data frame in R?") |>   chat(ollama())  get_reply(reply) ## [1] "A tibble is a modern reimagining of the data frame from the tidyverse. The main ## differences are that tibbles never convert strings to factors, never change column ## names, and print only the first ten rows by default, showing column types beneath ## the header. title: Local Models with tidyllm
text: Installation. macOS, install via Homebrew: Linux, build source use pre-built release llama.cpp GitHub releases page. Windows, pre-built binaries available page; pick variant matches hardware (GPU-accelerated CUDA Nvidia Vulkan AMD/Intel, CPU-dedicated GPU). Starting server. Unlike Ollama, llama.cpp manage models : point GGUF file disk serves model stop process. server command llama-server: -c flag sets context window size tokens (32768 good default tasks). started, server listens http://localhost:8080 default. Embeddings require separate process. embedding endpoint uses dedicated embedding model must run different port: can run commands separate terminal tabs, keep running background using terminal multiplexer like tmux. Telling tidyllm look. Add following .Renviron file (run usethis::edit_r_environ() open , restart R): LLAMACPP_SERVER address tidyllm uses reach chat server. LLAMACPP_MODEL_DIR folder model files stored management functions look .
code: brew install llama.cpp llama-server -m ~/models/Qwen3.5-8B-Q4_K_M.gguf -c 32768 llama-server \   -m ~/models/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf \   -c 512 --port 8081 --embeddings LLAMACPP_SERVER=http://localhost:8080 LLAMACPP_MODEL_DIR=~/models

what: Confidence scores for annotation quality control
title: Local Models with tidyllm
text: use model classification annotation, predictions equally confident. Log-probabilities give numeric confidence score output token: score close zero means model highly certain; strongly negative score means genuinely uncertain several options. Passing .logprobs = TRUE stores scores response metadata alongside reply: score -0.85 “neutral” means model genuine uncertainty; review mixed model knows . large annotation batch, can flag predictions winning score better -1 (high confidence) direct use, route rest larger model human review. Log-probability scores also available openai() ollama().
code: result <- llm_message(     "Reply with exactly one word. Classify the sentiment of this review:      'The delivery was two days late but the product itself is solid.'"   ) |>   chat(llamacpp(.logprobs = TRUE, .top_logprobs = 3))  get_reply(result) get_metadata(result)$api_specific$logprobs ## [1] "neutral" ## $content ## $content[[1]] ## $content[[1]]$token ## [1] "neutral" ## $content[[1]]$logprob ## [1] -0.847 ## $content[[1]]$top_logprobs ## # A tibble: 3 × 2 ##   token    logprob ##   <chr>      <dbl> ## 1 neutral   -0.847 ## 2 mixed     -1.20  ## 3 positive  -2.03

what: Grammar constraints for reliable structured output
title: Local Models with tidyllm
text: Instruction-tuned models generally follow format instructions well, can occasionally produce malformed output, especially smaller models unusual inputs. Grammar constraints enforce output format deepest level model’s text generation process: model physically prevented producing tokens violate grammar, regardless size well follows instructions. means even small model running laptop always return valid JSON pass .json_schema. .grammar parameter goes : accepts grammar definition can constrain output pattern, just JSON:
code: sentiment_grammar <- r"(root ::= ("positive" | "negative" | "neutral"))"  result <- llm_message(     "Classify the sentiment of this review. Reply with one word only:      'Fast shipping, exactly as described, would buy again.'"   ) |>   chat(llamacpp(.grammar = sentiment_grammar))  get_reply(result) ## [1] "positive"

title: Using Tools with tidyllm
text: model without tools access live data: model knows Stuttgart Europe/Berlin timezone, call Sys.time(). attach tool, workflow becomes: model receives prompt together tool descriptions. identifies external data needed. sends tool call request back R session. tidyllm runs R function returns result model. model generates final response using tool result. Steps 3-5 happen automatically inside chat(). define function parameter schema.
code: library(tidyllm)  llm_message("What time is it in Stuttgart right now?") |>   chat(openai()) ##  ## Attaching package: 'tidyllm' ## The following object is masked from 'package:stats': ##  ##     embed ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## What time is it in Stuttgart right now? ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## I don't have access to real-time information, so I cannot ## tell you the current time in Stuttgart. Please check a clock ## or a time zone website for the current local time. ## --------------------------------------------------------------

title: Using Tools with tidyllm
text: tidyllm_tool() wraps R function schema model can read: Pass single tool .tools = time_tool list tools .tools = list(tool_a, tool_b).
code: get_current_time <- function(tz, format = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") {   format(Sys.time(), tz = tz, format = format, usetz = TRUE) }  time_tool <- tidyllm_tool(   .f           = get_current_time,   .description = "Returns the current time in a specified timezone.",   tz     = field_chr("The timezone identifier, e.g. 'Europe/Berlin'."),   format = field_chr("strftime format string. Default: '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'.") )  llm_message("What time is it in Stuttgart right now?") |>   chat(openai(), .tools = time_tool) ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## What time is it in Stuttgart right now? ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## The current time in Stuttgart (Europe/Berlin timezone) is ## 2025-03-03 09:51:22 CET. ## --------------------------------------------------------------

title: Using Tools with tidyllm
text: parameter function needs matching schema entry. field helpers correspond R types: function several parameters different types: field_fct() particularly useful: passing .levels, tell model exactly values valid, prevents inventing category names.
code: query_products <- function(category, max_price, in_stock_only = TRUE) {   products |>     filter(       category == category,       price <= max_price,       if (in_stock_only) stock > 0 else TRUE     ) }  product_tool <- tidyllm_tool(   .f           = query_products,   .description = "Query the product catalogue by category and price.",   category     = field_fct(     "Product category to filter on.",     .levels = c("electronics", "clothing", "books", "home")   ),   max_price    = field_dbl("Maximum price in USD."),   in_stock_only = field_lgl("If TRUE, only return products currently in stock.") )

title: Using Tools with tidyllm
text: tools plain R functions, access everything session: data frames, database connections, API wrappers, file system paths. following example lets model answer questions employee records stored tibble: model formulates query, receives returned text, composes natural-language answer.
code: library(tidyverse)  employees <- tribble(   ~id, ~name,           ~department,   ~salary,   1L,  "Alice Müller",  "Engineering", 82000,   2L,  "Bob Schmidt",   "Marketing",   65000,   3L,  "Clara Fischer", "Engineering", 91000,   4L,  "David Braun",   "HR",          58000 )  get_employee <- function(department) {   employees |>     filter(department == department) |>     select(name, salary) |>     purrr::pmap_chr(\(name, salary) glue::glue("{name}: ${salary}")) |>     paste(collapse = "\n") }  employee_tool <- tidyllm_tool(   .f           = get_employee,   .description = "Return name and salary for all employees in a department.",   department   = field_fct(     "The department to query.",     .levels = c("Engineering", "Marketing", "HR")   ) )  llm_message("Who works in Engineering and what do they earn?") |>   chat(claude(), .tools = employee_tool) ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## Who works in Engineering and what do they earn? ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## The Engineering department has two employees: ##  ## - **Alice Müller** earns $82,000 ## - **Clara Fischer** earns $91,000 ## --------------------------------------------------------------

title: Using Tools with tidyllm
text: R packages can expose ready-made tool definitions. definitions written ellmer, convert tidyllm ellmer_tool(): ellmer_tool() also supports provider-native builtin tools. example, Claude’s built-web search exposed ellmer can passed directly chat():
code: library(ellmer)  btw_tool <- ellmer_tool(btw::btw_tool_files_list_files)  llm_message("List the files in the R/ folder.") |>   chat(claude(), .tools = btw_tool) ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## List the files in the R/ folder. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## The `R/` folder contains the following files: ##  ## - `LLMMessage.R` ## - `APIProvider.R` ## - `llm_verbs.R` ## - `llm_message.R` ## - `tools.R` ## - `tidyllm_schema.R` ## - `api_openai.R` ## - `api_claude.R` ## - `api_gemini.R` ## - `api_ollama.R` ## - `zzz.R` ## -------------------------------------------------------------- web_search <- ellmer_tool(ellmer::claude_tool_web_search())  llm_message("What are the latest developments in R package tooling?") |>   chat(claude(), .tools = web_search)

title: Using Tools with tidyllm
text: solving problem requires one tool call, model make sequence. round, tidyllm executes requested function, returns result, model decides whether another call needed can answer. .max_tool_rounds argument (default 10) caps number rounds prevent runaway loops: model first called get_country_capital("Japan") get Tokyo, called get_city_population("Tokyo") get figure, finally composed answer. Passing list tools lets model choose one call step.
code: get_country_capital <- function(country) {   capitals <- c(     France  = "Paris",     Germany = "Berlin",     Japan   = "Tokyo",     Brazil  = "Brasilia"   )   unname(capitals[country]) %||% "Unknown" }  get_city_population <- function(city) {   populations <- c(     Paris    = "2.1 million (city), 12 million (metro)",     Berlin   = "3.7 million (city), 6 million (metro)",     Tokyo    = "14 million (city), 37 million (metro)",     Brasilia = "3.1 million (city)"   )   unname(populations[city]) %||% "Unknown" }  capital_tool <- tidyllm_tool(   .f           = get_country_capital,   .description = "Return the capital city of a country.",   country      = field_chr("Country name, e.g. 'France'.") )  population_tool <- tidyllm_tool(   .f           = get_city_population,   .description = "Return the population of a city.",   city         = field_chr("City name, e.g. 'Paris'.") )  llm_message("What is the population of the capital of Japan?") |>   chat(openai(), .tools = list(capital_tool, population_tool), .max_tool_rounds = 5) ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## What is the population of the capital of Japan? ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## The capital of Japan is **Tokyo**. Its population is ## approximately 14 million people in the city proper and ## around 37 million in the greater metropolitan area, making ## it the most populous metropolitan area in the world. ## --------------------------------------------------------------

title: Using Tools with tidyllm
text: models can issue multiple tool calls single round, waiting results. tidyllm executes returns results together, reduces number API round-trips independent lookups: Instead three sequential rounds (call, return, call, return, call, return), model issued three calls , tidyllm executed single batch continuing.
code: get_weather <- function(city) {   weather <- c(     Paris  = "15°C, partly cloudy",     London = "9°C, overcast with light rain",     Tokyo  = "22°C, clear and sunny"   )   unname(weather[city]) %||% "No data available" }  weather_tool <- tidyllm_tool(   .f           = get_weather,   .description = "Return the current weather for a city.",   city         = field_chr("City name.") )  llm_message("What is the weather like in Paris, London, and Tokyo right now?") |>   chat(openai(), .tools = weather_tool) ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## What is the weather like in Paris, London, and Tokyo right ## now? ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## Here is the current weather for each city: ##  ## - **Paris**: 15°C, partly cloudy ## - **London**: 9°C, overcast with light rain ## - **Tokyo**: 22°C, clear and sunny ##  ## Tokyo has the warmest and sunniest conditions, while London ## is the coolest with some rain. ## --------------------------------------------------------------

title: Using Tools with tidyllm
text: tidyllm_tool() wraps R function tool definition model can discover call. field_chr(), field_dbl(), field_lgl(), field_fct() describe parameter types; field_fct() .levels constrains model valid values. Tools can access anything R session: -memory data, databases, APIs, file system. ellmer_tool() converts ellmer ToolDef objects provider-native builtin tools tidyllm format, tools packages like btw work without modification. Multi-step chains handled automatically; .max_tool_rounds prevents runaway loops. Models support parallel tool calls batch independent lookups single round; tidyllm handles execution transparently.

title: Working with Files and Media

title: Working with Files and Media
text: .media list can contain combination types. Gemini handles mixed content flexibly — can send images, PDFs, audio, video together single message. ask model cross-reference figure paper came : combine chart audio commentary : item list processed order items end message. provider sees one multi-part input alongside text prompt.

Does it accurately represent the results in Table 2?",             .media = list(               img("figure_3.png"),               pdf_file("paper.pdf", pages = 1:8)             )) |>   chat(gemini()) llm_message("The audio is my verbal annotation of this chart. Summarise both together.",             .media = list(               img("quarterly_results.png"),               audio_file("my_notes.mp3")             )) |>   chat(gemini())

title: Working with Files and Media
text: Use pdf_file() attach PDF. providers support native PDF rendering (Claude Gemini), file sent -, model can see tables, figures, formatting just human reader . providers, tidyllm automatically extracts text passes model instead. Either way, write code: always use text extraction path regardless provider, pass .text_extract = TRUE. useful want consistent behaviour across providers document text-heavy layout matter: Limit pages sent model pages argument:
code: llm_message("Summarise the key findings from this report.",             .media = pdf_file("annual_report.pdf")) |>   chat(claude()) llm_message("Summarise the key findings.",             .media = pdf_file("annual_report.pdf", .text_extract = TRUE)) |>   chat(openai()) llm_message("What does the introduction argue?",             .media = pdf_file("paper.pdf", pages = 1:5)) |>   chat(claude())

text: particularly compelling use case working scanned PDFs contain machine-readable text . Text extraction return nothing useful, sending binary file Claude Gemini lets model read actual page images. makes possible process old journal scans, archival documents, PDF created photographs rather typeset text. use scanned copy 1995 economics paper ask Claude extract references APA format: approach works handwritten notes, printed forms, historical records, document visual layout carries meaning text extraction lose.

(1989). Job displacement, relative wage changes, and duration of unemployment. Journal of Labor Economics, 7(3), 281-302." ## [2] "Altonji, J., & Shakotko, R. (1987). Do wages rise with seniority? title: Working with Files and Media
text: audio video, use audio_file() video_file(). Gemini broadest native support handles common formats (MP3, WAV, MP4, MOV, ). OpenRouter routes audio video underlying models accept ; can filter audio video modality openrouter.ai/models. Local models via llama.cpp support audio compatible model files (Ultravox, Qwen2.5-Omni, Qwen3-Omni, Gemma 4 variants). examples section use two pieces archival material: MP3 recording interview Robert Bosch describing early history company, 1960s TV documentary segment artificial intelligence called Thinking Machine, featuring Claude Shannon early AI researchers. Video works way:
code: # Describe or transcribe an audio recording llm_message("Summarise what is discussed in this recording.",             .media = audio_file("bosch_interview.mp3")) |>   chat(gemini()) ## Message History: ## system: ## You are a helpful assistant ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## user: ## Summarise what is discussed in this recording. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- ## assistant: ## This is an audio recording of an interview with Robert ## Bosch. He discusses his early life, his path from apprentice ## to founder of his renowned company, and his philosophy on ## business. Bosch recounts his decision to become a precision ## mechanic, his travels to America and England, and the ## development of his famous magneto ignition system. He ## emphasises principles of high-quality work, fair treatment ## of employees, and the importance of trust and reliability in ## business dealings. ## -------------------------------------------------------------- # Route to a Gemini model via OpenRouter llm_message("Summarise this recording.",             .media = audio_file("bosch_interview.mp3")) |>   chat(openrouter(.model = "google/gemini-2.5-flash")) llm_message("Give a two-sentence summary of what happens in this video.",             .media = video_file("documentary.mp4")) |>   chat(gemini())

title: Working with Files and Media
text: can combine video JSON schema extract specific fields clip, just text. useful cataloguing video archives extracting metadata scale. define schema short documentary clip ask Gemini populate : get_reply_data() returns named list matching schema. Wrap as_tibble() get single-row data frame, use purrr::map_dfr() process whole folder clips tidy table one go. files around 20 MB audio 100 MB video, sending inline fine. larger files, use Files API described next section.
code: video_schema <- tidyllm_schema(   title       = field_chr("The title or subject of the clip, as best you can tell"),   era         = field_chr("The approximate decade or period depicted"),   format      = field_fct("What type of content is this?",                            .levels = c("Documentary", "Interview", "Lecture", "News", "Other")),   key_people  = field_chr("Names of people who appear or are mentioned, semicolon-separated"),   main_topics = field_chr("The two or three main topics covered, semicolon-separated") )  result <- llm_message("Analyse this video clip and fill in the schema.",                       .media = video_file("the_thinking_machine.mp4")) |>   chat(gemini(), .json_schema = video_schema)  get_reply_data(result) ## $title ## [1] "The Thinking Machine" ##  ## $era ## [1] "1960s" ##  ## $format ## [1] "Documentary" ##  ## $key_people ## [1] "Claude Shannon; unnamed narrator" ##  ## $main_topics ## [1] "Artificial intelligence; machine learning; early computers"

title: tidyllm
text: tidyllm provides pipeline-oriented, functional interface working LLM APIs data analysis workflows. Unlike agent-focused packages, tidyllm emphasizes: Batch processing cost-efficient large-scale LLM operations Provider-specific features packages consider --scope Immutable message objects pipeline safety Tidy data outputs (tibbles) seamless integration tidyverse workflows Relationship ellmer: complementary, competitive. tidyllm targets data pipelines, batch operations, provider-specific power features. ellmer targets interactive agents, Shiny apps, async use cases.

title: tidyllm
text: API interactions follow: verb(.llm, provider()) Verbs: chat(), embed(), send_batch(), check_batch(), fetch_batch(), list_batches(), list_models(), deep_research(), check_job(), fetch_job(), upload_file(), list_files(), file_info(), delete_file() check_job() / fetch_job(): type-dispatching aliases — delegate check_batch()/fetch_batch() batch objects (batch_id attr), provider-specific research functions tidyllm_research_job objects (dispatches job$provider: "openai" → openai_check_research/openai_fetch_research; "perplexity" → perplexity_check_research/perplexity_fetch_research) upload_file() / list_files() / file_info() / delete_file(): remote file verbs — manage files stored provider servers (Claude, Gemini, OpenAI); providers error clear message Providers: openai(), claude(), gemini(), ollama(), mistral(), groq(), perplexity(), deepseek(), voyage(), azure_openai(), openrouter(), llamacpp(), chat_completions(), mixedbread() (planned) Batch providers (support send/check/fetch/list): Claude, OpenAI, Mistral, Groq, Gemini Deep research providers: Perplexity (sonar-deep-research via async endpoint); OpenAI (o3-deep-research, o4-mini-deep-research via Responses API background mode)

title: tidyllm
text: create_provider_function() creates provider function. called verb, verb: 1. Evaluates provider expression get call 2. Calls provider(.called_from = "metadata") get supported argument names 3. Filters common args supported provider 4. Injects via rlang::call_modify evals result means chat(openai(), .thinking = TRUE) correctly errors .thinking openai_chat’s formals. provider’s chat function authoritative source supports. shared dispatch_to_provider(provider_expr, verb_name, common_args, validate) helper llm_verbs.R implements logic — verbs delegate . Use validate = FALSE verbs like check_batch()/fetch_batch()/list_batches() don’t need metadata lookup.

title: tidyllm
text: LLMMessage: Immutable conversation history container. History stored list message objects @message_history. message content, media (inline media: base64-encoded binary read disk message construction time), files (remote file references: tidyllm_file objects pointing files stored provider’s servers). APIProvider: Base class @short_name, @long_name, @api_key_env_var. Provides default implementations generics. TOOL: Tool/function definition. @func (R function), @input_schema (named list tidyllm_field), @builtin (provider-native tools like Claude web search). tidyllm_field: Schema field descriptor @type, @description, @enum, @vector, @schema (nested objects). tidyllm_image: Inline image; returned img(). Base64-encoded eagerly construction; stored message$media; provider-agnostic. tidyllm_audio: Inline audio; returned audio_file(). Stores path MIME ; binary read base64-encode deferred to_api_format(). tidyllm_video: Inline video; returned video_file(). lazy-encoding pattern audio. tidyllm_pdf: Inline PDF; returned pdf_file(). Stores path + pdftools text extraction construction; to_api_format() uses binary supported, silently falls back tagged text otherwise. tidyllm_file: Remote file reference; returned upload_file(). @id, @provider, @mime_type, @filename, @uri. Stored message$files. Provider-specific: Claude file sent Gemini; mismatch validated to_api_format() time.

title: tidyllm
text: Live-API package. Prefer real non-mocked local tests. Keep local_tests/. goes : - Mocked tests (tests/testthat/): basic chat + embed shape checks . CRAN-safe. - Local tests (local_tests/): everything live — tool use, streaming, async, structured output, file uploads, provider-specific features. Tool use async must stay . Structure: Running tests: source("local_tests/providers/claude.R") run single provider suite. Feature tests: source("local_tests/features/gemini_file_api.R") run_feature_tests(). Async batch workflow: source("local_tests/batch_submit.R") → wait → source("local_tests/batch_fetch.R"). Claude Code reads: local_tests/results/latest_run.json see pass/fail state. llama.cpp servers: bash local_tests/start_llamacpp_servers.sh — starts chat (port 8080) embed (port 8081) servers; idempotent, skips ports already use.
code: local_tests/ ├── run_tests.R              # run_all_local_tests(providers=NULL); run_feature_tests() ├── test_harness.R           # llt_test(), llt_expect_*(), llt_report() ├── async_tracker.R          # save/load/check async job state; job_is_pending() ├── batch_submit.R           # opt-in: submit plain + json_schema batches per provider (idempotent) ├── batch_fetch.R            # opt-in: fetch completed batch jobs and assert results ├── start_llamacpp_servers.sh  # start chat (8080) + embed (8081) servers; idempotent ├── media/                   # Test assets (images, PDFs, audio) — tracked in git ├── providers/               # Per-provider suites — source one after changing a provider │   ├── claude.R             # chat, stream, tools, thinking (no batch — moved to batch_submit/fetch) │   ├── openai.R             # chat, stream, tools, vision, reasoning_effort, stateful, built-in tools │   ├── chat_completions.R   # generic chat_completions() provider smoke tests │   ├── gemini.R             # chat, tools, embed, list_models │   ├── perplexity.R         # chat, streaming, citations, search_recency_filter │   ├── ellmer.R             # chat_ellmer (anthropic + openai), ellmer_tool(), claude_websearch() │   ├── openrouter.R         # chat, streaming, multi-turn, tools, list_models, fallback routing │   ├── llamacpp.R           # chat, streaming, multi-turn, tools, json_schema, logprobs, model management │   └── ...                  # one file per provider ├── features/                # Opt-in suites for paid/stateful or slow features │   ├── gemini_grounding.R   # .grounding_threshold, groundingMetadata in api_specific │   ├── gemini_file_api.R    # upload/chat/metadata/list/delete — on.exit cleanup │   ├── claude_file_api.R    # same lifecycle for Claude Files API │   ├── openai_file_api.R    # same lifecycle for OpenAI Files API │   ├── inline_media.R       # img(), audio_file(), pdf_file(), multi-image — cross-provider │   ├── perplexity_deep_research.R  # deep_research() blocking + background; slow (5-10 min) │   └── openai_deep_research.R      # OpenAI o4-mini-deep-research; slow (5-30 min) ├── async_state/             # Gitignored — live batch job objects (.rds) ├── debug_state/             # Gitignored — dump.frames() post-mortem dumps (.rda) └── results/                 # Gitignored — JSON results for Claude Code to read     └── latest_run.json      # Written by llt_report() after each run

title: tidyllm
text: R’s browser() requires TTY driven Claude Code. Use post-mortem debugging instead — harness writes two artifacts every failure: Inline traceback latest_run.json — read first, usually enough. local variables captured stack unwinding. Query frame : developing new feature, run relevant provider suite significant change read latest_run.json proceeding. Don’t wait end find something broken. See local_wip/notes/local_testing_strategy.md full design. add mocking-based tests without consulting user first. adding new feature, create extend local_tests/providers/{provider}.R using llt_test() wrappers.

title: tidyllm
text: Inline vs. text-extracted PDFs: pdf_file() always extracts text construction time fallback. Providers listed “text path ” automatically use fallback; special handling needed. Providers native PDF support (Claude, Gemini) use binary encoding instead. Audio/video format details: Gemini supports WAV/MP3/AIFF/AAC/OGG/FLAC audio; MP4/MPEG/MOV/AVI/WebM video (inline 20MB/100MB; larger via File API). OpenRouter forwards input_audio video_url blocks underlying model — audio/video capable models growing list including Gemini, Gemma 4 (E2B/E4B), Qwen 3.6, others; filter modality openrouter.ai/models. tidyllm always sends object format {data, format} input_audio via OpenRouter (plain string). Audio wire format differences: Gemini OpenRouter use {data: "<b64>", format: "mp3"} objects inside input_audio. Mistral uses plain string ("<b64>" URL). llama.cpp uses object format OpenAI/OpenRouter. tidyllm implementation status: Audio currently implemented gemini() openrouter(). Mistral llama.cpp audio support planned upcoming 0.5.x release — wire formats known providers already carry format difference image implementations.

title: tidyllm
text: async/await: Intentional — batch APIs tidyllm’s preferred async pattern. Ollama default models require pull: Default chat (qwen3.5:4b) embed (qwen3-embedding:0.6b) require ollama pull qwen3.5:4b && ollama pull qwen3-embedding:0.6b. .imagefile deprecated 0.5.0: Use .media = img(path) instead. Emits deprecation warning. .file_ids / .fileid deprecated 0.5.0: Use upload_file(provider()) + .files llm_message() instead. OpenAI Files API image limitation: upload_file(openai()) accepts images Responses API supports document types (input_file blocks); use inline img() OpenAI image vision. Cross-provider chains audio/video: Audio/video content blocks silently ignored providers don’t support . chaining Gemini (audio leg) → Claude (text continuation), audio item prior message dropped to_api_format() time — Claude sees text content.

title: tidyllm
text: Current release: 0.4.0 (submitted CRAN 2026-03-17) development: 0.5.0 Phase 1 (OpenAI Provider Split) complete: - api_chat_completions class (renamed api_openai); chat_completions() provider OAI-compatible endpoint - api_openai rewrote Responses API (POST /v1/responses); generics implemented - OpenAI deep_research() via background=true; check_job()/fetch_job() provider-aware dispatch - .stateful = TRUE openai_chat() server-side context via previous_response_id - Built-tools: openai_websearch(), openai_code_interpreter() (openai_file_search() omitted: queries vector stores, tidyllm yet manage) Phase 2 (Files Media System) complete: - Four new S7 media types R/media.R: tidyllm_audio, tidyllm_video, tidyllm_pdf, tidyllm_file - Constructors: audio_file(), video_file(), pdf_file() (lazy encoding); img() unchanged (eager) - llm_message() gains .media .files; .imagefile .pdf deprecated warnings - format_message() returns images list (multi-image); providers updated iterate - add_message() gains .files parameter; print.LLMMessage shows S7 media labels file refs - Four new file verbs: upload_file(), list_files(), file_info(), delete_file() - validate_message_attachments() called chat() provider mismatch detection - Claude: upload_file() returns tidyllm_file; file verbs registered; to_api_format() handles multi-image, PDF text fallback, message$files - Gemini: ; columns normalized file_id/filename/size_bytes/mime_type/created_at/expires_at/uri - OpenAI Responses API: upload_file() returns tidyllm_file; to_api_format() emits input_image input_file blocks - ChatCompletions: to_api_format() updated multi-image; Files API (errors message) - Ollama, Ellmer: to_api_format() updated iterate formatted$images - OpenRouter: to_api_format() handles tidyllm_audio (input_audio block) tidyllm_video (video_url block) addition images - Live tests: local_tests/features/claude_file_api.R, gemini_file_api.R, openai_file_api.R, inline_media.R (video + cross-provider chain included)

title: Send LLM Messages to an Azure OpenAI Chat Completions endpoint — azure_openai_chat
text: .llm LLMMessage object containing conversation history. .endpoint_url Base URL API (default: Sys.getenv("AZURE_ENDPOINT_URL")). .deployment identifier model deployed (default: "gpt-4o-mini"). .api_version version API deployed (default: "2024-08-01-preview"). .max_completion_tokens upper bound number tokens can generated completion. .reasoning_effort long reasoning models reason (can either "low","medium" "high"). .frequency_penalty Number -2.0 2.0. Positive values penalize new tokens based existing frequency. .logit_bias named list modifying likelihood specified tokens appearing completion. .presence_penalty Number -2.0 2.0. Positive values penalize new tokens based whether appear text far. .seed specified, system make best effort sample deterministically. .stop 4 sequences API stop generating tokens. .stream set TRUE, answer streamed console comes (default: FALSE). .temperature sampling temperature use, 0 2. Higher values make output random. .top_p alternative sampling temperature, called nucleus sampling. .timeout Request timeout seconds (default: 60). .verbose additional information shown API call (default: FALSE). .json_schema JSON schema object provided tidyllm schema ellmer schemata. .max_tries Maximum retries perform request. .dry_run TRUE, perform dry run return request object (default: FALSE). .logprobs TRUE, get log probabilities output token (default: NULL). .top_logprobs specified, get top N log probabilities output token (0-5, default: NULL). .tools Either single TOOL object list TOOL objects representing available functions tool calls. .tool_choice character string specifying tool-calling behavior; valid values "none", "auto", "required". .max_tool_rounds Integer specifying maximum number tool use iterations (default: 10).

title: Send LLM Messages to an Azure OpenAI Chat Completions endpoint — azure_openai_chat
text: new LLMMessage object containing original messages plus assistant's response.

what: Chat with a Language Model — chat

title: Chat with a Language Model — chat
text: .llm LLMMessage object containing message conversation history send language model. .provider function function call specifying language model provider additional parameters. call provider function like openai(), claude(), etc. can also set default provider function via tidyllm_chat_default option. .dry_run Logical; TRUE, simulates request without sending provider. Useful testing. .stream Logical; TRUE, streams response provider real-time. .temperature Numeric; controls randomness model's output (0 = deterministic). .timeout Numeric; maximum time (seconds) wait response. .top_p Numeric; nucleus sampling parameter, limits sampling top cumulative probability p. .max_tries Integer; maximum number retries failed requests. .model Character; model identifier use (e.g., "gpt-4"). .verbose Logical; TRUE, prints additional information request response. .json_schema List; JSON schema object R list enforce output structure .tools Either single TOOL object list TOOL objects representing available functions tool calls. .max_tool_rounds Integer; maximum number tool use iterations multi-turn tool calling (default varies provider). .seed Integer; sets random seed reproducibility. .stop Character vector; specifies sequences model stop generating tokens. .frequency_penalty Numeric; adjusts likelihood repeating tokens (positive values decrease repetition). .presence_penalty Numeric; adjusts likelihood introducing new tokens (positive values encourage novelty).

title: Chat with a Language Model — chat
text: updated LLMMessage object containing response language model.

title: Chat with a Language Model — chat
text: chat() function provides unified interface interacting different language model providers. Advanced provider-specific configurations can accessed via provider functions.

text: .llm LLMMessage object containing conversation history system prompt. .model Character string specifying Claude model version (default: "claude-sonnet-4-6"). .max_tokens Integer specifying maximum number tokens response (default: 1024). .temperature Numeric 0 1 controlling response randomness. .top_k Integer controlling diversity limiting top K tokens. .top_p Numeric 0 1 nucleus sampling. .metadata List additional metadata include request. .stop_sequences Character vector sequences halt response generation. .tools List additional tools functions model can use. .json_schema schema enforce output structure .file_ids Character; vector file IDs files uploaded Anthropics Servers .api_url Base URL Anthropic API (default: "https://api.anthropic.com/"). .verbose Logical; TRUE, displays additional information API call (default: FALSE). .max_tries Maximum retries peform request .timeout Integer specifying request timeout seconds (default: 60). .stream Logical; TRUE, streams response piece piece (default: FALSE). .dry_run Logical; TRUE, returns prepared request object without executing (default: FALSE). .thinking Logical; TRUE, enables Claude's thinking mode complex reasoning tasks (default: FALSE). .thinking_budget Integer specifying maximum tokens Claude can spend thinking (default: 1024). Must least 1024. .max_tool_rounds Integer specifying maximum number tool use iterations (default: 10). Set 1 single-round tool use, higher multi-turn agentic loops.

text: .llm LLMMessage object containing conversation history. .model identifier model use (default: "llama-3.2-11b-vision-preview"). .max_tokens maximum number tokens can generated response (default: 1024). .temperature Controls randomness model's response. Values 0 2 allowed, higher values increase randomness (optional). .top_p Nucleus sampling parameter controls proportion probability mass considered. Values 0 1 allowed (optional). .frequency_penalty Number -2.0 2.0. Positive values penalize repeated tokens, reducing likelihood repetition (optional). .presence_penalty Number -2.0 2.0. Positive values encourage new topics penalizing tokens appeared far (optional). .stop One sequences API stop generating tokens. Can string list strings (optional). .seed integer deterministic sampling. specified, attempts return result repeated requests identical parameters (optional). .tools Either single TOOL object list TOOL objects representing available functions tool calls (optional). .tool_choice character string specifying tool-calling behavior; valid values "none", "auto", "required" (optional). .api_url Base URL Groq API (default: "https://api.groq.com/"). .json_schema list tidyllm schema created tidyllm_schema() structured JSON output (optional). .timeout Request timeout seconds (default: 60). .verbose TRUE, displays additional information API call, including rate limit details (default: FALSE). .stream Logical; TRUE, streams response piece piece (default: FALSE). .dry_run TRUE, performs dry run returns constructed request object without executing (default: FALSE). .max_tries Maximum retries peform request .max_tool_rounds Integer specifying maximum number tool use iterations (default: 10).
